name: Cranberry Vaccinium macrocarpon
summary: It is best framed around proanthocyanidin-rich urinary-support positioning rather than broad antimicrobial claims.
description: It is best framed around proanthocyanidin-rich urinary-support positioning rather than broad antimicrobial claims.
safetyNotes: Generally well tolerated for most users.

sources.title: Cranberry Polyphenols and Prevention against Urinary Tract Infections: Relevant Considerations
sources.authors: González de Llano D et al.

sources.title: Safety and efficacy of cranberry (vaccinium macrocarpon) during pregnancy and lactation
PubMed PMID 37068952. Minimal citation row added from existing workbook PMID; title/year/journal still require PubMed metadata extraction.

sources.note: evidence citation lookup; PMID source present in Evidence_Register

claimMap.claim: v10.0: Cochrane 2023 found cranberry products may reduce symptomatic culture-verified UTIs in women with recurrent UTIs, children, and post-intervention bladder-susceptible groups, but not elderly institutionalized adults, neuromuscular bladder, incomplete emptying, or pregnant women.
claimMap.notes: v10.0: Cochrane 2023 found cranberry products may reduce symptomatic culture-verified UTIs in women with recurrent UTIs, children, and post-intervention bladder-susceptible groups, but not elderly institutionalized adults, neuromuscular bladder, incomplete emptying, or pregnant women.

qualifiers.dose_or_duration: Product-specific cranberry dose; PAC content should be disclosed, commonly targeting 36 mg PAC/day in UTI-focused products

dosage: Juice: 240-300 mL/day; standardized extract (~36 mg proanthocyanidins/day); variability across products
typical_dosage: Juice: 240-300 mL/day; standardized extract (~36 mg proanthocyanidins/day); variability across products
